summary: Gruber argues that Apple consistently prioritizes obviousness over simplicity and elegance in its interface design. He uses the evolution of the iPhone's lock screen camera shortcut from iOS 5.0 to 5.1 as a case study: the original double-tap-to-reveal design was elegant but undiscoverable, so Apple replaced it with an always-visible slide-up button. He draws an analogy between gestures on iOS and keyboard shortcuts on the Mac — both are conveniences for power users, but the default interaction should always be visual and obvious. The core principle is that Apple would rather sacrifice elegance than leave users unable to find a feature.
key_points: Gestures on iOS serve the same role as keyboard shortcuts on Mac OS: convenient alternatives for advanced users, not the primary interaction method
The iOS 5.0 lock screen camera shortcut required a double-tap to reveal, which Gruber himself — despite knowing the feature existed — couldn't find without asking someone
Apple changed the camera shortcut in iOS 5.1 to an always-visible button, trading elegance for discoverability
Double-anything interactions are inherently problematic for usability because most users remain confused by them
The slide-up camera button includes a clever bounce animation when tapped, visually teaching users the correct gesture without requiring documentation
money_quotes: Gestures are to iOS what keyboard shortcuts are to Mac OS — an alternative way to do something as a convenience for advanced users.
The default, true way to do things should be visual.
The sliding camera button on the iOS 5.1 home screen is a perfect example of Apple favoring obviousness over simplicity and even elegance.
I only found it because I knew it was supposed to be there.
A reduction in elegance, but an increase in obviousness.
Double-anything is generally problematic usability-wise — consider how many people you know who remain confused about single- vs. double-clicking mouse buttons on the Mac and Windows.
If you do just tap on it, which is an obvious thing to try, you get a clever hint: the lock screen playfully jumps and bounces, suggesting visually that you can slide it upward.
key_insight: When forced to choose between elegance and discoverability, Apple will always sacrifice elegance — because a feature users can't find might as well not exist.
